◑ GROWING NOTE algorithmscs-fundamentalscourse-notes

Course Notes — DSA

Running notes for a Data Structures & Algorithms course — concepts, analysis methods, and worked examples.

PLANTED · 1 AUGUST 2025 · ZETTEL NOTE

Running notes for a Data Structures and Algorithms course. Updated as topics progress.

Basic Concepts

A Computational Problem asks for a solution in terms of an Algorithm.

The Algorithm maps a set of instances or cases to a possibly empty set of solutions — so a computational problem is a relation that maps instances to solutions.

The behavior of algorithms is described using model of computations, which specify how an algorithm handles input/output and what operations it may perform. This course uses the RAM Model of Computation.

Formally, an algorithm A⊆I×OA \subseteq I \times OA⊆I×O solves a computational problem if:

  • It takes inputs i∈Ii \in Ii∈I
  • It produces outputs o∈Oo \in Oo∈O
  • The produced pair (i,o)∈A(i, o) \in A(i,o)∈A

A Data Structure stores and organizes data for efficient algorithm access.

Analysis of Algorithms

While analyzing an algorithm, we evaluate its efficiency and correctness. The algorithm should:

  1. Solve the problem correctly for all valid inputs
  2. Utilize resources efficiently — primarily time, secondarily space

Primitive Operations

To analyze at an abstract level, we count primitive operations — each defined to run in O(1)O(1)O(1) time on the RAM model:

  • Arithmetic operations (+, −, ×, ÷, mod)
  • Comparison and assignment of variables
  • Array indexing
  • Logical operations
  • Function calls and pointer dereferencing

Growth of Functions

We study running time T(n)T(n)T(n) as a function of input size nnn. Three asymptotic notations bound growth:

Big-O (upper bound)

f(n)∈O(g(n))f(n) \in O(g(n))f(n)∈O(g(n)) if there exist constants c>0c > 0c>0 and n0n_0n0​ such that: f(n)≤c⋅g(n)for all n≥n0f(n) \leq c \cdot g(n) \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0f(n)≤c⋅g(n)for all n≥n0​

We say fff grows no faster than ggg.

Big-Omega (lower bound)

f(n)∈Ω(g(n))f(n) \in \Omega(g(n))f(n)∈Ω(g(n)) if there exist constants c>0c > 0c>0 and n0n_0n0​ such that: f(n)≥c⋅g(n)for all n≥n0f(n) \geq c \cdot g(n) \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0f(n)≥c⋅g(n)for all n≥n0​

We say fff grows at least as fast as ggg.

Big-Theta (tight bound)

f(n)∈Θ(g(n))f(n) \in \Theta(g(n))f(n)∈Θ(g(n)) if f(n)∈O(g(n))f(n) \in O(g(n))f(n)∈O(g(n)) and f(n)∈Ω(g(n))f(n) \in \Omega(g(n))f(n)∈Ω(g(n)).

fff and ggg grow at the same rate up to constant factors.

Common Complexity Classes

ClassNameTypical algorithm
O(1)O(1)O(1)ConstantArray access, hash lookup
O(log⁡n)O(\log n)O(logn)LogarithmicBinary search, balanced BST ops
O(n)O(n)O(n)LinearLinear scan, BFS
O(nlog⁡n)O(n \log n)O(nlogn)LinearithmicMerge sort, heap sort
O(n2)O(n^2)O(n2)QuadraticInsertion sort, bubble sort
O(n3)O(n^3)O(n3)CubicMatrix multiplication (naive)
O(2n)O(2^n)O(2n)ExponentialSubset enumeration
O(n!)O(n!)O(n!)FactorialPermutation enumeration

Sorting Algorithms

Insertion Sort

  • Best case: Θ(n)\Theta(n)Θ(n) — already sorted
  • Worst case: Θ(n2)\Theta(n^2)Θ(n2) — reverse sorted
  • Space: O(1)O(1)O(1) in-place
  • Stable: yes

Works well for small nnn or nearly-sorted data. Inner loop is tight and cache-friendly.

Merge Sort

  • All cases: Θ(nlog⁡n)\Theta(n \log n)Θ(nlogn)
  • Space: O(n)O(n)O(n) — requires auxiliary array
  • Stable: yes

Divide-and-conquer: split in half, sort each half, merge. The merge step takes O(n)O(n)O(n); the recurrence is T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n)T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n), which solves to O(nlog⁡n)O(n \log n)O(nlogn) by the Master Theorem.

Heap Sort

  • All cases: O(nlog⁡n)O(n \log n)O(nlogn)
  • Space: O(1)O(1)O(1) in-place
  • Stable: no

Build a max-heap in O(n)O(n)O(n), then extract-max nnn times in O(log⁡n)O(\log n)O(logn) each.

Data Structures Covered

StructureAccessSearchInsertDelete
ArrayO(1)O(1)O(1)O(n)O(n)O(n)O(n)O(n)O(n)O(n)O(n)O(n)
Linked ListO(n)O(n)O(n)O(n)O(n)O(n)O(1)O(1)O(1)O(1)O(1)O(1)
Stack / QueueO(n)O(n)O(n)O(n)O(n)O(n)O(1)O(1)O(1)O(1)O(1)O(1)
Hash Table—O(1)O(1)O(1) avgO(1)O(1)O(1) avgO(1)O(1)O(1) avg
AVL TreeO(log⁡n)O(\log n)O(logn)O(log⁡n)O(\log n)O(logn)O(log⁡n)O(\log n)O(logn)O(log⁡n)O(\log n)O(logn)
HeapO(n)O(n)O(n)—O(log⁡n)O(\log n)O(logn)O(log⁡n)O(\log n)O(logn)

Related

  • Algorithm — formal definition
  • Computational Problem — what we’re solving
  • RAM Model of Computation — the machine model
  • Data Structure — how data is organized
